walkthrough.title: Download the App
walkthrough.text: Let's get Werewords ready to play! The first thing we need is the free Werewords app on a phone or tablet. This app is required because it manages the secret word, the timer, and the night phase narration. Go ahead and download it now if you have not already.

walkthrough.title: Choose Roles and Player Count
walkthrough.text: Now let's pick our roles. Open the app and enter the number of players. For seven players, we recommend one Mayor, one Seer, one Werewolf, and four Villagers. For larger groups, you can add optional roles like the Beholder or Minion to spice things up.

walkthrough.title: Deal Role Tokens
walkthrough.text: Let's hand out the roles. Shuffle the role tokens face down and deal one to each player. Everyone looks at their token secretly without revealing it to anyone. Remember which role you got because it determines your goal for the entire round.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Answer Tokens
walkthrough.text: Place the Yes, No, Maybe, So Close, and Correct tokens in the center of the table within easy reach of whoever is the Mayor. These tokens are the only way the Mayor can communicate during the game, so make sure they are clearly visible to everyone.

walkthrough.title: Configure the App
walkthrough.text: For your first game, we recommend easy or medium difficulty with a broad category like "things" or "random." The app will pick the secret word from your chosen settings. Once everyone is ready, hit start to begin the night phase.

summary.title: Download the App
bullets: Free Werewords app required (phone or tablet)
Manages secret word, timer, and night narration

summary.title: Choose Roles and Player Count
bullets: Enter player count in app
7p rec: 1 Mayor, 1 Seer, 1 Werewolf, 4 Villagers
Optional: add Beholder, Minion, or 2nd Werewolf for larger groups

summary.title: Deal Role Tokens
bullets: Shuffle role tokens face-down → deal 1 per player
View secretly, do not reveal

summary.title: Set Up the Answer Tokens
bullets: Place Yes / No / Maybe / So Close / Correct tokens centrally
Must be within Mayor's reach

summary.title: Configure the App
bullets: Set word difficulty (easy/medium rec for first game)
Choose category or use random
Press start → night phase begins

walkthrough.title: Night Phase Overview
walkthrough.text: Let's walk through how a round begins. The app guides the night phase with audio narration. Everyone closes their eyes. Then the Mayor, Werewolf, and Seer each wake up one at a time to secretly see the word. The Beholder and Minion also wake if they are in the game.

walkthrough.title: Who Knows What
walkthrough.text: Here is the key information split. The Mayor, Seer, and Werewolf all know the secret word. Villagers do not know the word at all. The Seer wants to help the village guess the word while staying hidden. The Werewolf wants to prevent the guess while also staying hidden.

walkthrough.title: Day Phase: Asking Questions
walkthrough.text: Once everyone opens their eyes, the timer starts. Any player can ask the Mayor a yes-or-no question about the secret word. The Mayor responds only by placing a Yes, No, Maybe, or So Close token. There is no strict turn order, so just speak up when you have a question.

walkthrough.title: Guessing the Word
walkthrough.text: At any point during the day phase, a player can attempt to guess the secret word out loud. If they guess correctly, the Mayor places the Correct token and the day phase ends immediately. If the guess is wrong, the group keeps asking questions until the timer runs out.

walkthrough.title: The Mayor's Limitations
walkthrough.text: The Mayor cannot speak at all during the day phase. Their only tools are the physical tokens. They also have a limited supply of each token type, so the group should be strategic about the questions they ask. When a token type runs out, the Mayor can no longer give that answer.

walkthrough.title: Endgame: Word Guessed
walkthrough.text: If the village guesses the word, the Werewolf team gets one chance to identify the Seer. There is a brief discussion, and then the Werewolf picks one player they think is the Seer. If the Werewolf guesses correctly, the Werewolf team wins despite the word being solved. If the Werewolf is wrong, the village team wins.

walkthrough.title: Endgame: Timer Expires
walkthrough.text: If the timer runs out without the word being guessed, the village team gets one chance to identify the Werewolf. Everyone discusses who asked suspicious or misleading questions, then the group votes. If they correctly identify the Werewolf, the village wins anyway. If they vote wrong, the Werewolf team wins.

walkthrough.title: Reveal and Reset
walkthrough.text: After the round ends, everyone reveals their role tokens and the app shows the secret word. Then shuffle the role tokens, re-deal them, and start a new round. Most groups play several rounds in a row, informally tracking how many times each team wins.

summary.title: Night Phase Overview
bullets: App narrates night phase → all eyes closed
Mayor, Werewolf, Seer wake individually to see the word

bullets: Know the word: Mayor, Seer, Werewolf
Don't know: Villagers (+ Beholder, Minion)
Seer helps village; Werewolf hinders village

Wrong guess → keep asking

summary.title: Endgame: Word Guessed

Correct Seer ID → Werewolf team wins
Wrong Seer ID → Village team wins

summary.title: Endgame: Timer Expires
bullets: Word not guessed → Village tries to identify the Werewolf
Correct Werewolf ID → Village team wins
Wrong Werewolf ID → Werewolf team wins

summary.title: Reveal and Reset
bullets: Reveal all roles + the secret word
Reshuffle tokens → deal → play another round
Informally track team wins across rounds

walkthrough.title: Welcome to Werewords
walkthrough.text: We will walk through a practice round with seven players. The roles are Mayor, Seer, Werewolf, and four Villagers. The app has selected the secret word, and the night phase is complete. Let's open our eyes and start guessing.

walkthrough.title: Starting with Broad Questions
walkthrough.text: The timer is running. Villager Alice asks the Mayor: Is it a living thing? The Mayor places a No token. Villager Bob follows up: Is it something you can find indoors? The Mayor places a Yes token. Great, we are already narrowing things down quickly.

walkthrough.title: The Seer Subtly Guides
walkthrough.text: The Seer, Carol, knows the word is "lamp." She asks: Is it something you use every day? The Mayor places a Yes token. Notice how Carol is nudging the group toward household items without being too obvious. If she asked "Does it give off light?" that would narrow it too fast and make her a target.

walkthrough.title: The Werewolf Misleads
walkthrough.text: The Werewolf, Dave, also knows the word is "lamp." He asks: Is it electronic, like a phone or computer? The Mayor places a Maybe token. Dave is trying to steer the group toward thinking about screens and gadgets instead of lighting. It sounds like a reasonable question, so nobody suspects him.

walkthrough.title: Beginner Mistake: Seer is Too Obvious
walkthrough.text: Carol gets excited and asks: Can you put it on a nightstand? The Mayor places Yes. Then Carol immediately follows with: Does it have a shade on top? The Mayor places Yes again. Two very specific, very helpful questions back to back. The Werewolf Dave takes mental note. Carol is almost certainly the Seer.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Spread Out Your Helpful Questions
walkthrough.text: If you are the Seer, never cluster your most helpful questions together. Ask one guiding question, then let other people ask a few questions before you help again. The Werewolf is watching for exactly this pattern, and being identified as the Seer can cost the village the game even after guessing the word.

walkthrough.title: The Village Closes In
walkthrough.text: Villager Eve asks: Is it a lamp? The Mayor places the Correct token. The village guessed the word with time to spare. But the round is not over yet. Now the Werewolf gets a chance to identify the Seer.

walkthrough.title: Werewolf Identifies the Seer
walkthrough.text: Dave thinks back to the questions. Carol asked about nightstands and shades, two very targeted clues. Dave points at Carol and says she is the Seer. He is correct. The Werewolf team wins this round even though the village guessed the word. Carol's helpful questions gave her away.

walkthrough.title: Beginner Mistake: Not Watching Other Players
walkthrough.text: Villager Frank admits he was so focused on guessing the word that he never considered who was asking helpful versus unhelpful questions. Observing question quality is essential because the endgame always involves identifying a hidden role, whether it is the Seer or the Werewolf.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Always Observe While You Guess
walkthrough.text: Even as a Villager, you have two jobs at the same time. First, ask good questions to narrow down the word. Second, pay attention to which players are being especially helpful or suspiciously unhelpful. This dual awareness is what separates beginners from experienced Werewords players.

walkthrough.title: Round Two Preview
walkthrough.text: In the next round, Carol plays the Werewolf and learns from her mistake. She asks reasonable-sounding questions that gently mislead the group without being obvious. Meanwhile, the new Seer spaces out helpful questions across the entire timer. The village fails to guess the word, but they correctly identify Carol as the Werewolf because her questions were slightly off. Observation pays off.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: You have seen how a full round of Werewords plays out, including both endgame scenarios. You learned that the Seer should spread out helpful clues and that every player should observe question patterns alongside guessing the word.

walkthrough.title: Ready to Play
walkthrough.text:
Before you start your real game, here are three things to remember.

First, check out General Tips for strategies on guiding, misleading, and observing.

Second, reference the Appendix for a quick rundown of all roles and terms.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

walkthrough.title: Start with Binary Search Questions
walkthrough.text: The fastest way to narrow down the secret word is to ask questions that cut the possibilities in half. Start with big categories like "Is it alive?" or "Can you hold it in one hand?" Each answer should eliminate roughly half of all remaining options. Save specific questions for later when you are close.

walkthrough.title: Seer Strategy: The Slow Drip
walkthrough.text: As the Seer, your goal is to help the village guess the word without the Werewolf figuring out who you are. Space your helpful questions throughout the round. Ask one guiding question, then stay quiet for two or three other questions before helping again. Let other players get credit for the breakthrough guess.

walkthrough.title: Werewolf Strategy: Plausible Misdirection
walkthrough.text: As the Werewolf, ask questions that sound legitimate but point in the wrong direction. If the word is "piano," ask something like "Is it smaller than a backpack?" This sounds reasonable but steers the group toward small objects. Avoid asking obviously bad questions because they make you easy to identify.

walkthrough.title: Mayor Strategy: Use So Close Wisely
walkthrough.text: Save it for moments when a guess is extremely near the correct answer. If someone says "light" and the word is "lamp," a So Close token tells the whole group they are on the right track without revealing the exact word.

walkthrough.title: Track Question Quality
walkthrough.text: Pay close attention to whose questions actually advance the group's progress. The Seer's questions tend to be suspiciously productive. The Werewolf's questions tend to be subtly unhelpful. This observation is critical because the endgame always requires identifying one of these roles.

walkthrough.title: Manage the Timer
walkthrough.text: You have about four minutes per round. If you are halfway through the timer and still stuck on basic categories, speed up and take more risks with your guesses. A wrong guess costs nothing. Running out of time and failing to identify the Werewolf costs the village the game.

walkthrough.title: Everyone Should Ask Questions
walkthrough.text: Both the Seer and the Werewolf need to ask questions to blend in. If one player stays completely silent, they become suspicious by default. Even if you are unsure what to ask, throw out a basic category question to maintain cover and keep the conversation flowing.

walkthrough.title: Difficulty Scaling
walkthrough.text: Adjust the word difficulty to match your group. Easy words on your first few rounds help everyone learn the social deduction elements without getting stuck on hard vocabulary. Once your group is comfortable identifying the Seer and Werewolf, increase the difficulty to make the guessing phase more challenging.

summary.title: Start with Binary Search Questions
bullets: Ask questions that halve the possibilities
Broad categories first → specific details later

summary.title: Seer Strategy: The Slow Drip
bullets: Space helpful questions across the round
Let 2-3 other questions pass between your hints
Avoid getting credit for the breakthrough

summary.title: Werewolf Strategy: Plausible Misdirection
bullets: Ask reasonable questions that point in the wrong direction
Avoid obviously bad questions → draws suspicion
Subtlety > aggression

summary.title: Mayor Strategy: Use So Close Wisely
bullets: Save So Close for near-miss guesses
Powerful signal that village is on the right track
Don't overuse Maybe — commit to Yes/No when possible

summary.title: Track Question Quality
bullets: Seer questions = suspiciously productive
Werewolf questions = subtly unhelpful
Observation is critical for endgame identification

bullets: ~4 min per round — speed up if stuck at halfway
Wrong guesses cost nothing
Running out + failing Werewolf ID = village loss

bullets: Silence = suspicion
Seer and Werewolf must ask to blend in
Even basic questions maintain cover

summary.title: Difficulty Scaling
bullets: Start easy → learn social deduction first
Increase difficulty once group reads roles well
Hard words make guessing tenser, not deduction

walkthrough.title: Seer Misdirection
walkthrough.text: As an advanced Seer, occasionally ask a question that seems unhelpful to throw the Werewolf off your trail. If the word is "bicycle," ask "Is it made of wood?" to make yourself look like a clueless Villager. Then subtly guide the group with your next question a few turns later.

walkthrough.title: Werewolf Credit Stealing
walkthrough.text: A clever Werewolf can appear to be the Seer by asking one genuinely helpful question early on. This makes you look like a village ally. Later, if the village guesses the word and needs to protect the Seer, players might mistakenly protect you instead of the real Seer.

walkthrough.title: Mayor as Werewolf
walkthrough.text: In some configurations, the Mayor can also be the Werewolf. This is extremely powerful because the Mayor controls the Yes and No tokens. A Werewolf Mayor can give subtly misleading answers. Occasionally answering No when the truth is Yes can send the village chasing in the wrong direction entirely.

walkthrough.title: The Beholder Gambit
walkthrough.text: If the Beholder role is in play, the Beholder knows who the Seer is but not the word. An advanced Beholder can echo the Seer's line of questioning to amplify their hints without either player being too obvious. This teamwork spreads the suspicion across two players instead of one.

walkthrough.title: Timing Your Guess
walkthrough.text: If you think you know the word, consider the timing carefully. Guessing immediately after a Seer-like question draws a direct line between the guesser and the helper. Wait a beat, ask a neutral question, then guess on your next turn. This makes it harder for the Werewolf to trace the assist back to the Seer.

walkthrough.title: Token Counting
walkthrough.text: Keep a mental count of how many Yes and No tokens the Mayor has used. When tokens run low, the village might be forced into blind guessing. A Werewolf can exploit this by asking rapid-fire questions to burn through the token supply faster, leaving the village with less information for the final stretch.

walkthrough.title: Reading the Mayor's Hesitations
walkthrough.text: Advanced players watch the Mayor's body language before they place a token. A brief hesitation before placing a Maybe might mean the answer is actually Yes but the Mayor is unsure how to express a nuance. These micro-signals can give observant players an edge in interpreting ambiguous answers.

walkthrough.title: Multi-Round Meta
walkthrough.text: Over multiple rounds, players develop reputations. If you played a quiet Werewolf last round, players might suspect you when you are quiet again. Vary your play style each round. Ask different numbers of questions, change your energy level, and avoid creating a predictable pattern that opponents can read.

summary.title: Seer Misdirection
bullets: Occasionally ask an unhelpful question to blend in
Makes you look like a Villager to the Werewolf

summary.title: Werewolf Credit Stealing
bullets: Ask 1 helpful question early to look like the Seer
Village may protect you instead of the real Seer

summary.title: Mayor as Werewolf
bullets: Werewolf Mayor can give misleading token answers
Subtle wrong answers derail the entire village

summary.title: The Beholder Gambit
bullets: Beholder echoes Seer's line of questioning
Spreads suspicion across 2 players instead of 1

summary.title: Timing Your Guess
bullets: Don't guess immediately after a Seer hint
Wait a beat → ask neutral question → then guess
Breaks the line between helper and guesser

summary.title: Token Counting

summary.title: Reading the Mayor's Hesitations

entries.definition: The player who knows the secret word and answers questions using only Yes, No, Maybe, and So Close tokens. Cannot speak during the day phase.
A village-team player who knows the secret word and tries to subtly guide the group toward guessing it without being identified by the Werewolf.
A player who knows the secret word and tries to prevent the village from guessing it while staying hidden. If the word is guessed, the Werewolf can still win by identifying the Seer.
A village-team player who does not know the secret word. Asks questions and tries to guess the word while observing other players for suspicious behavior.
An optional village-team role that knows who the Seer is but does not know the secret word. Can support the Seer without being the primary target.
An optional Werewolf-team role that knows who the Werewolf is. Tries to help derail guessing and protect the Werewolf's identity.
The word selected by the app that the village is trying to guess. Known to the Mayor, Seer, and Werewolf during the night phase.
The app-guided phase at the start of each round where all players close their eyes and specific roles wake up to receive information.
The timed phase where players ask the Mayor yes-or-no questions and attempt to guess the secret word before time runs out.
A token the Mayor places to confirm a question about the secret word. Limited supply per round.
A token the Mayor places to deny a question about the secret word. Limited supply per round.
A token the Mayor places when a question is ambiguous or partially true. Should be used sparingly to avoid confusing the village.
A token the Mayor places when a guess or question is very near the correct answer. A powerful signal that the village is on the right track.
A token the Mayor places when a player guesses the exact secret word. Ends the day phase immediately and triggers the Seer identification phase.
The Mayor, Seer, Villagers, and Beholder. They win if the word is guessed and the Seer is not identified, or if the word is missed but the Werewolf is identified.
